passage:
Text 1
Researchers studying urban parks have found that access to green spaces is linked to lower reported stress levels among city residents. A 2022 survey of 800 adults living in major cities found that those who visited a park at least once per week were 35 percent more likely to describe their overall stress as "low" compared to those who never visited parks.

Text 2
The same research team tracked weekly park visits and self-reported stress ratings (on a scale of 1 to 10, where 1 is least stressed) for 200 participants over six months. Their data showed the following average stress ratings by visit frequency:

| Weekly Park Visits | Average Stress Rating |
|---|---|
| 0 visits | 7.2 |
| 1–2 visits | 5.8 |
| 3–4 visits | 4.5 |
| 5+ visits | 3.9 |

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from urban wellness research study materials

stem: Which statement is best supported by both texts taken together?

choices.label: A
choices.text: More frequent park visits are associated with lower stress ratings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People who visit parks have no stress at all.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stress decreases only after five or more park visits per week.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Park visits affect stress levels only in adults over age 40.

explanation: Text 1 establishes that visiting a park at least once per week correlates with lower self-reported stress. Text 2 provides quantitative support: as weekly park visits increase from 0 (average stress 7.2) to 1-2 (5.8) to 3-4 (4.5) to 5+ (3.9), stress ratings consistently decrease. Together, the two sources confirm that higher visit frequency is associated with lower stress across the entire range of data.

distractorRationale.B: Text 2 shows that even those who visit 5 or more times per week still report an average stress rating of 3.9 out of 10, not zero stress. This distractor overgeneralizes the relationship.
distractorRationale.C: The data in Text 2 shows stress decreasing at every frequency level starting from 1-2 visits, not only at 5+ visits. This distractor misreads the pattern by focusing only on the highest frequency category.
distractorRationale.D: Neither text mentions participant age as a variable. This distractor introduces a factor (age 40+) that has no basis in either source.
